note_data.title: Algebra
keyPoints: **Quadratic equations** can be solved by factorisation, completing the square, or using the quadratic formula
The **discriminant** b² - 4ac determines the nature of roots: positive gives real distinct roots, zero gives equal roots, negative gives no real roots
For **inequalities**, reverse the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing by a negative number
**Linear simultaneous equations** can be solved by elimination or substitution method
**Polynomial division** uses long division or synthetic division to find quotient and remainder
**Factor theorem** states that (x - a) is a factor of f(x) if and only if f(a) = 0
**Remainder theorem** states that when polynomial f(x) is divided by (x - a), the remainder is f(a)
**Quadratic inequalities** are solved by finding critical values and testing intervals on a number line
**Partial fractions** decompose rational expressions into simpler fractions for integration or simplification

definitions.definition: The expression b² - 4ac in a quadratic equation ax² + bx + c = 0, which determines the nature of the roots
If f(a) = 0, then (x - a) is a factor of polynomial f(x)
When polynomial f(x) is divided by (x - a), the remainder equals f(a)
x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac))/2a for solving ax² + bx + c = 0

howToAnswer.instruction: Find all values that satisfy the equation or inequality, showing clear algebraic steps
Write as a product of factors in simplest form
Calculate the exact value using appropriate algebraic methods
Use the previous result directly in your solution

modelAnswer.question: Solve the inequality x² - 5x + 6 < 0
modelAnswer.answer:
x² - 5x + 6 = 0
(x - 2)(x - 3) = 0
x = 2 or x = 3
Testing intervals: x < 2 gives positive, 2 < x < 3 gives negative, x > 3 gives positive
Therefore 2 < x < 3
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Finding critical values x = 2 and x = 3

commonMistakes: Never forget to reverse inequality sign when multiplying/dividing by negative — always check the sign
Students often write x = 2, 3 instead of 2 < x < 3 for quadratic inequalities
Never use quadratic formula when factorisation is simpler — examiners expect the most efficient method
Students confuse factor theorem and remainder theorem — factor gives zero, remainder gives f(a)
Never leave answers as decimals when exact form is required — use surds or fractions
examinerTips: Quadratic equations appear in every paper — master all three solution methods
Always show substitution clearly when using factor theorem
Draw number line for inequality questions to avoid sign errors
Check solutions by substitution back into original equation
formulas: Sum of roots = -b/a, Product of roots = c/a
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Number line showing critical points and sign testing intervals for quadratic inequalities, with open/closed circles indicating included/excluded values
